data.title: Stellantis confirms 2026 guidance as it targets sequential North America margin improvement quarter-by-quarter
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Stellantis (STLA) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Following the decisive reset action taken in '25, our focus is now on disciplined execution, and we are seeing early signs of progress, consistent with our expectations." (CEO Antonio Filosa)
  • "Quarter 1, '26, we are now seeing the results of that successful execution as we delivered a return to profitability." (CEO Filosa)
  • "In North America, despite a challenging U.S. market where the industry was down 6%, our sales increased 4%, driven by Ram and by Jeep." (CEO Filosa)
  • "We gained approximately 80 basis points of market share." (CEO Filosa)
  • "Ram specifically had a very strong quarter 1, posting a 20% U.S. sales increase year-over-year." (CEO Filosa)
  • "Our EU30 market share reached 17.5%." (CEO Filosa)
  • "Adding the sales from Leapmotor, our combined market share in Europe increased to 18.1%." (CEO Filosa)
  • "Consolidated shipments were 1.4 million units, up 12% year-over-year, with all regions contributing to the growth." (Chief Financial Officer Joao Laranjo)
  • "Net revenues were EUR 38.1 billion." (CFO Laranjo)
  • "Adjusted operating income returned to positive at EUR 1 billion for the quarter." (CFO Laranjo)
  • "Industrial free cash flow was negative EUR 1.9 billion in Q1." (CFO Laranjo)

Outlook

  • "Looking ahead to the remainder of the year, we are confirming our 2026 financial guidance as outlined on February 6." (CFO Laranjo)
  • "We expect an improvement in net revenues, margins and industrial free cash flow supported by strong liquidity and a more resilient operating model." (CFO Laranjo)
  • "We expect to improve margins quarter-by-quarter sequentially this year in North America." (CEO Filosa)
  • "We recently launched a global program of cost management that is called VCP, value creation program." (CEO Filosa)

Financial Results

  • "Net revenues were EUR 38.1 billion, up EUR 2.3 billion or 6% compared to Q1 of last year." (CFO Laranjo)
  • "AOI margin was 2.5%, representing a 160 basis point improvement year-over-year." (CFO Laranjo)
  • "The impact of tariffs was broadly neutral year-over-year as the recognition of approximately EUR 400 million of IEEPA tariff adjustment offset Q1 2026 tariff costs." (CFO Laranjo)
  • "In March 2026, we issued 3 tranches of hybrid perpetual notes for a total of EUR 5 billion." (CFO Laranjo)
  • "Industrial available liquidity of EUR 44 billion at quarter end." (CFO Laranjo)
  • "Total inventory increased 11% year-over-year to 1.3 million units." (CFO Laranjo)
  • "North America delivered a positive AOI of EUR 263 million with an AOI margin of 1.6%." (CFO Laranjo)
  • "In Enlarged Europe, AOI was effectively breakeven." (CFO Laranjo)

Q&A

  • Jose Asumendi, JPMorgan: "Do you think Q2 earnings will be up in the U.S. versus Q1?" CEO Filosa: "What we strongly expect for quarter 2 is to keep on that trajectory and deliver a quarter 2, which is better than quarter 2 prior year for sure."
  • Jose Asumendi, JPMorgan: working capital and restructuring cash outflow. CFO Laranjo: "Working capital, it's seasonal" and "we believe we'll have a more balanced free cash flow generation in 2026."
  • Thomas Besson, Kepler Cheuvreux: CapEx and what’s needed for North America margins to recover. CFO Laranjo: "CapEx for this year, we expect to be slightly below 7% of net revenues." CEO Filosa: "we will keep further improving" using "price discipline" and "working a lot on cost."
  • Stuart Pearson, Oxcap Analytics: sequential improvement vs a weaker prior-year base and industrial cost path. CEO Filosa: "yes, we expect to improve margins quarter-by-quarter sequentially this year in North America." CFO Laranjo: "we're also going to see -- start seeing some improvements in warranty as well."
  • Patrick Hummel, UBS: commodity headwinds vs IEEPA and Europe trajectory. CFO Laranjo: "it's slightly above the EUR 400 million IEEPA credits" and "it still has a minor headwind." CEO Filosa: "the focus of Europe will be to deliver sequential improvement as well keeping as North Star breakeven plus."
  • Gautam Narayan, RBC: sequential North America margins despite tariffs and commodities; Chinese partnerships in the U.S. CEO Filosa: "margins in North America... will improve sequentially quarter-by-quarter" and "No. The answer is not" regarding a "U.S.-oriented Chinese partnership."
  • Christian Frenes, Goldman Sachs: Ram mix durability, Europe pricing headwinds, Leapmotor profitability. CEO Filosa: "mix will be a lever all around the year"; on Leapmotor International, "it's profitable" and "we have sold 24,000 units in quarter 1."
  • Stephen Reitman, Bernstein: U.S. channel mix and Leapmotor cannibalization. CEO Filosa: "the fleet sales, they are back to historic level" and "we don't see so far risk of cannibalization."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to pressing, repeatedly pushing for clarity on sequential North America margins and cash generation, including "just to be absolutely clear" (Stuart Pearson, Oxcap Analytics) and detailed challenges on tariffs, commodities, and Europe pricing.
  • Management was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more assertive in Q&A on North America, using explicit commitments such as "we expect to improve margins quarter-by-quarter sequentially" (CEO Filosa) while also emphasizing uncertainty and monitoring in specific areas, including "we need to keep monitoring any evolution of the current situation" (CEO Filosa).
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s emphasis that "'26 is our year of execution" and "we expect to see progressive performance across all our business KPIs" (CEO Filosa, Q4 2025), the current call used more concrete near-term language on sequential North America margin improvement and reiterated "confirming our 2026 financial guidance" (CFO Laranjo) without changing it.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 introduced a more explicit North America cadence, with "we expect to improve margins quarter-by-quarter sequentially this year in North America" (CEO Filosa), while Q4 2025 framed performance as "progressive performance improvement on all of our business KPIs" (CEO Filosa) without the same quarter-by-quarter margin commitment.
  • Q1 2026 added detail on financing and balance sheet actions, including "we issued... hybrid perpetual notes for a total of EUR 5 billion" (CFO Laranjo), while Q4 2025 focused more heavily on the reset actions, segment reporting changes, and the move to quarterly reporting.
  • Analyst focus tightened in Q1 2026 toward North America margin mechanics, commodity impacts, and Europe pricing persistence; in Q4 2025, questions more often centered on restructuring, operating leverage, CapEx trajectory, and when cash flow would turn positive.

Risks and Concerns

  • "The environment remains challenging and across all regions" (CEO Filosa), with management pointing to volatility in "geopolitical, trading and inflationary pressures" (CFO Laranjo).
  • On commodities, "there is a lot of volatility" and "the full impact could be -- could approach close to 1% of revenue" if current prices persist (CFO Laranjo).
  • On Europe, management cited regulatory pressure in light commercial vehicles: "regulation is forcing an unattainable mix of BEV into light commercial vehicle" (CEO Filosa), and positioned mitigation around costs: "we launched this massive cost management program" (CEO Filosa).

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 2026 as an execution-driven inflection, highlighting "a return to profitability" (CEO Filosa) alongside confirmed full-year guidance (CFO Laranjo). The call concentrated investor attention on a specific North America margin path—"improve margins quarter-by-quarter sequentially" (CEO Filosa)—supported by mix, pricing discipline, and the newly launched VCP cost program, while also acknowledging commodity volatility and Europe’s regulatory and pricing headwinds.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Titan outlines Q2 2026 adjusted EBITDA of $25M-$30M amid Iran conflict cost headwind
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Titan International (TWI) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Our first quarter marked a solid start to the year with revenues and adjusted EBITDA near the high end of our guidance ranges.” (CEO, President & Director Paul Reitz)
  • “Looking ahead to Q2, included in our guidance is an approximately $3 million headwind in operating margins due to the impact of the war in Iran.” (CEO Reitz)
  • “Sales grew 2.9% year-over-year… Gross margins improved to 14.1% and adjusted EBITDA grew to… $31 million.” (Senior VP & Chief Financial Officer Anthony Eheli)
  • “A strategic event of note during the quarter was our decision to close our Jackson, Tennessee plant.” (CFO Eheli)

Outlook

  • “Our guidance for the quarter is revenues of $470 million to $490 million and adjusted EBITDA of $25 million to $30 million.” (CFO Eheli)
  • “For fiscal year 2026, our financial guidance remains unchanged and is as follows: revenues of $1.85 billion to $1.95 billion and adjusted EBITDA of $105 million to $115 million.” (CFO Eheli)
  • “Versus last year, that guidance implies some top line growth, along with modest reduction in bottom line performance compared with last year's Q2.” (CFO Eheli)
  • “The primary factors driving the bottom line reduction are OEM pricing pressure and additional cost pressure… related to the Iran conflict.” (CFO Eheli)

Financial Results

  • “Segment revenues were up 11% to $160 million.” (CFO Eheli)
  • “SG&A, including R&D, was $57.7 million compared to $54.4 million for the comparable period in prior year.” (CFO Eheli)
  • “Operating cash used during the first quarter was $47 million.” (CFO Eheli)
  • “CapEx in the first quarter was $13 million compared to $15 million in the prior year period.” (CFO Eheli)
  • “Free cash flow was negative $60 million with… net debt at quarter end of $441 million and a leverage ratio of 4.3x.” (CFO Eheli)
  • “We recorded nonrecurring restructuring expense of approximately $25 million… the vast majority, approximately $23 million was noncash.” (CFO Eheli)

Q&A

  • “Could you give us a little more detail on Ag markets in Europe and the rest of South America compared to North America?” (Linda Umwali, D.A. Davidson & Co., Research Division) — CEO Reitz said Europe has been “more stable through time,” adding, “we've been able to pick up some nice sized wins,” while Brazil has “short-term impacts” from political uncertainty.
  • “What are the OEMs telling you about 2027 at this point?” (Umwali, D.A. Davidson) — CEO Reitz answered, “it's still a little bit early,” and said discussions get more serious “past Q2 into the second half of the year.”
  • “Have the Section 232 tariff changes… made any impact on your pricing and orders for 2026?” (Umwali, D.A. Davidson) — CEO Reitz said, “net-net, we should see a positive… from the 232 changes.”
  • “Do you have any of your chemicals or… raw materials sourced or pass through from that region?” (Umwali, D.A. Davidson) — CFO Eheli replied, “The short answer is no.”
  • “What can hold [EMC] up… versus what are the risks given all the geopolitical concerns?” (Steve Ferazani, Sidoti & Company, LLC) — CEO Reitz pointed to Europe supply-chain positioning and said the main watch item is “primarily the German OEMs,” where forecasts have been “more volatile.”
  • “Is that what I was hearing… margin… pressured because of inflation in 2Q and then you catch up…?” (Ferazani, Sidoti) — CFO Eheli said, “it's timing,” and added, “for the rest of the year, we don't expect to see much of an impact.”
  • “Any more restructuring or impairment charges… in the second quarter?” (Joseph Gomes, NOBLE Capital Markets, Inc., Research Division) — CFO Eheli said, “Not impairments, just restructuring expenses,” tied to the Jackson plant closure.
  • “What… investing in on the R&D side these days?” (Gomes, NOBLE Capital) — CEO Reitz highlighted a consumer product: “you can use it… and run it with no air at all,” and tied momentum to “the Goodyear brand.”
  • “Have you been hearing anything about additional farm subsidies…?” (Kirk Ludtke, Imperial Capital, LLC, Research Division) — CEO Reitz referenced administration support and said, “he just continues to reinforce the importance of making sure the farmer is taken care of.”
  • “Is there anything in that election that you think might fundamentally change the outlook for your Brazilian business?” (Ludtke, Imperial Capital) — CEO Reitz said, “We don't know,” and linked uncertainty to OEMs “take some weeks out of their schedule.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to neutral, pressing on geopolitics, tariffs, Brazil volatility, and the Q2 margin setup, including “lower than maybe what you were margin-wise 2Q” and questions on whether trade changes have “made any impact.” (Ferazani, Sidoti; Umwali, D.A. Davidson)
  • Management was slightly positive in prepared remarks but more guarded in Q&A on timing and volatility, emphasizing resilience and timing mechanics, including “we are confident in the durability of our business model” (CEO, President & Director Paul Reitz) and “it's timing” (CFO Eheli).
  • Compared with last quarter, management messaging shifted from “we are optimistic that… destocking has largely run its course” (CEO Reitz, Q4 call) toward more explicit near-term disruption framing, including “approximately $3 million headwind” tied to Iran (CEO Reitz, Q1 call).

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q1 results emphasized margin improvement and EMC growth, while Q2 guidance introduced a discrete Iran-related margin headwind; management said costs should be offset later: “we do expect the lion's share of the cost increases… to be directly offset with corresponding price increases.” (CEO Reitz)
  • Tariff discussion shifted from broad 2025 policy volatility to a specific mechanism in 2026: “Section 232… net-net… positive.” (CEO Reitz)
  • The capital structure tone tightened as management highlighted leverage and cash usage in Q1: “free cash flow was negative $60 million… leverage ratio of 4.3x.” (CFO Eheli)
  • Analysts’ focus moved from broad segment trajectory and cash flow/CapEx framing in Q4 to regional Ag divergence (Europe vs. Brazil), geopolitical supply-chain impacts, and the Q2 timing mismatch.

Risks and Concerns

  • “Continued headwinds we see in our end markets… due in part to the geopolitical developments that are out there.” (CEO Reitz)
  • “OEM pricing pressure” and Iran-driven cost inflation were cited as Q2 EBITDA pressures. (CFO Eheli)
  • Brazil demand risk was linked to politics and election uncertainty: “it's created uncertainty and uncertainty creates pullback in orders.” (CEO Reitz)
  • Execution risk around footprint actions was framed with timelines and expected benefits: “We expect to complete the closure by the end of October” and “annual cash savings of $5 million.” (CFO Eheli)

Final Takeaway

Titan’s management presented Q1 as a strong start with higher gross margin and $31 million of adjusted EBITDA, while flagging Q2 as a timing-driven margin reset from Iran-related input cost spikes and OEM contract lags. The company kept full-year guidance unchanged ($1.85 billion to $1.95 billion of revenue and $105 million to $115 million of adjusted EBITDA), announced the Jackson, Tennessee plant closure with expected annual cash savings, and repeatedly pointed to Europe share wins and EMC strength while describing Brazil as volatile due to election uncertainty and calling out German OEM variability as an EMC watch item.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: General Dynamics' subsidiary wins $716M deal for U.S. Army vehicle support
data.description:
• General Dynamics Land Systems, a unit of General Dynamics (GD), won a ~$716.2M contract for long-term sustainment services of U.S. Army vehicles, maintenance, field support, upgrades, training, and program management.
  • The work locations are not fixed, and funding will be allocated incrementally per order using relevant fiscal-year defense budgets.
  • The work is expected to be completed by Apr. 30, 2031, under Army Contracting Command oversight.

data.title: J&J ends development for several CAR-T large B-cell lymphoma programs
data.description:
• Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) said it is discontinuing development for its CD20 mono CAR-T cell therapy and CD19-CD20 bi-CAR-T programs, all for treatment of B-cell lymphoma.
  • "This is a strategic business decision based on the Company’s portfolio priorities and an assessment of the evolving large B-cell lymphoma treatment landscape," the company said in a statement.
  • J&J added that enrolled patients in trials "will continue to be supported."

data.title: Perimeter Solutions wins $500M fixed-price US defense logistics contract
data.description:
• Perimeter Solutions (PRM) has secured a fixed-price IDIQ contract worth up to ~$500M to supply aqueous film-forming foam, used in firefighting operations.
  • It is a 5-year contract with no extension options.
  • The order period is expected to end on April 29, 2031.
  • The foam will be supplied to key U.S. defense users, including the Army, Navy, and Coast Guard.
  • Funding will come from FY2026–FY2031 defense working capital funds, while the contract is being managed by the Defense Logistics Agency.

data.title: TriNet reiterates 2026 revenue $4.75B-$4.9B while tracking to top half of EPS guidance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: TriNet Group, Inc. (TNET) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "I'm pleased with our start to 2026." (President, CEO & Director Michael Simonds) "Our strong first quarter adjusted earnings per share up 25% over prior year reflect our disciplined approach to both repricing health fees and managing our expenses."
  • "Health fee repricing over the last year created a headwind for new sales and retention, including our January 2026 renewal, where attrition was about 2 points worse than prior year." (President, CEO & Director Simonds) "Despite the impact of our January repricing, we expect overall 2026 retention to be better than full year 2025."
  • "New sales grew modestly year-over-year in the first quarter." (President, CEO & Director Simonds) "For sales opportunities in the post proposal stage, we saw the time to close extend by about 15%."
  • "During the quarter, we completed the acquisition of Cocoon, an industry-leading employee leave management application aligned with our compliance-first approach." (President, CEO & Director Simonds) "Cocoon should integrate seamlessly into our platform and address a significant customer pain point."
  • "Next, we announced partnerships powering TriNet Global and TriNet IT." (President, CEO & Director Simonds) "TriNet Global powered through our partnership with Multiplier" and "TriNet IT powered through our partnership with Electric AI" were positioned as expansions to the offering.
  • "This March, we launched TriNet Assistant, an AI tool giving our customers and colleagues access to our HR expertise whenever and wherever needed." (President, CEO & Director Simonds) "Between March 31 and April 16, inbound volumes typically increase on average by 12%."
  • "Total revenues were $1.2 billion, declining 5% year-over-year in the first quarter as expected." (Executive VP & CFO Mala Murthy) "We finished the quarter with approximately 299,000 total WSEs, down 12% year-over-year."

Outlook

  • "We are reiterating our full year guidance." (Executive VP & CFO Murthy) "Revenue is performing in line with our forecast and our stronger than forecast Q1 insurance performance has had the effect of shifting our full year earnings expectations to the top half of our guidance range, assuming no significant uncontrollable event."
  • "For 2026, we continue to expect total revenues to be in the range of $4.75 billion to $4.9 billion." (Executive VP & CFO Murthy) "Our professional services revenue guidance remains in the range of approximately $625 million to $645 million, and our ICR remains in the range of 90.75% to 89.25%."
  • "Our adjusted EBITDA margin stays in the range of 7.5% to 8.7% and GAAP earnings per diluted share are in the range of $2.15 to $3.05 and adjusted earnings per diluted share in the range of $3.70 to $4.70." (Executive VP & CFO Murthy)
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s framing of repricing and retention, management now said, "With our January renewals complete, all cohorts within our customer base are now priced in line with more historical practices." (President, CEO & Director Simonds)

Financial Results

  • "For the first quarter, GAAP earnings per diluted share were $1.90, and adjusted net income per diluted share was $2.48." (Executive VP & CFO Murthy) "During the first quarter, we generated $186 million in adjusted EBITDA, representing an adjusted EBITDA margin of 15.2%."
  • "We generated $149 million in net cash provided by operating activities and $123 million in free cash flow." (Executive VP & CFO Murthy) "Free cash flow benefited from the 2025 tax law changes and timing of cash tax payments."
  • "Insurance costs in the first quarter declined by 9% year-over-year." (Executive VP & CFO Murthy) "As a result, our first quarter insurance cost ratio came in at 84% and over 4 points year-over-year improvement."
  • "Operating expenses were impacted by a $14 million restructuring charge as we right-sized the business for its current size and executed our ongoing talent optimization and automation strategies, including AI implementation." (Executive VP & CFO Murthy)
  • "We returned $71 million to shareholders across share repurchases and dividends." (Executive VP & CFO Murthy) "We also leveraged our cash generation to acquire Cocoon."
  • "Cocoon as a stand-alone product is expected to be modestly dilutive to 2026 adjusted earnings per diluted share and neutral to 2027 adjusted earnings per share." (Executive VP & CFO Murthy)

Q&A

  • Jared Levine, TD Cowen: asked whether March sales-cycle impacts were concentrated by vertical or client type; President, CEO & Director Simonds: "Yes, fairly broad-based" and added, "We saw a little bit more as you moved upmarket."
  • Jared Levine, TD Cowen: asked about Cocoon revenue opportunity and FY 2026 contribution; President, CEO & Director Simonds: "The primary benefit...is really about delivering better outcomes on these to our PEO clients first"; Executive VP & CFO Murthy: "the revenue contribution to this year is very, very modest."
  • Tyler Barishaw, Truist: asked if Cocoon was opportunistic and whether more M&A should be expected; President, CEO & Director Simonds: "we feel very fortunate to have such a strong franchise and such a cash-generative business" and said "inorganic is a lever that we can pull as well."
  • Daniel (for Andrew Nicholas), William Blair: pressed on ICR outperformance and guide conservatism; Executive VP & CFO Murthy: "The other half of the improvement was attributable to favorable development from 2025" and "that is a onetime benefit."
  • Daniel (for Andrew Nicholas), William Blair: asked when WSE declines trough; President, CEO & Director Simonds: "we can stabilize WSE count here for the balance of the year."
  • Brendan Biles, JPMorgan: asked what TriNet learned from the insurance repricing cycle and about AI-driven entrepreneurship; President, CEO & Director Simonds: "it's a risk-taking business" and said, "we've actually gone through and redone our rating system."
  • David Grossman, Stifel: asked where repriced clients go and what resonates in the broker channel; President, CEO & Director Simonds: "there hasn't been a big change in the distribution of where clients are going" and said brokers care "first and foremost about the experience and the value their client is going to get."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral, with probing on sales-cycle lengthening, ICR sustainability, WSE stabilization timing, and the practical implications of repricing; questions included "how we should think about the conservatism of the guide maintenance" (Daniel, for Andrew Nicholas, William Blair) and "where do these people go" (David Grossman, Stifel).
  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, emphasizing discipline and one-time items; Executive VP & CFO Murthy cautioned, "one quarter does not make a trend" and reiterated, "we believe it is prudent to maintain our full year range."
  • Versus last quarter, management’s confidence language shifted from previewing launches ("we will launch TriNet Assistant" (President, CEO & Director Simonds, Q4 2025)) to reporting operating impact ("we launched TriNet Assistant" (President, CEO & Director Simonds, Q1 2026)), while analysts continued to focus on repricing aftereffects and durability of insurance performance.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance posture stayed consistent, with Executive VP & CFO Murthy reiterating the same 2026 ranges in Q1 2026 that were introduced in Q4 2025, while adding that Q1 insurance performance "has had the effect of shifting our full year earnings expectations to the top half of our guidance range."
  • Strategic actions advanced from planned integrations in Q4 2025 ("We plan to announce new capabilities...and leave of absence" (President, CEO & Director Simonds)) to executed moves in Q1 2026 ("we completed the acquisition of Cocoon" and "we announced partnerships powering TriNet Global and TriNet IT" (President, CEO & Director Simonds)).
  • Analyst focus remained centered on repricing, retention, and WSE trajectories, but Q1 2026 included more scrutiny on ICR outperformance drivers and guide conservatism following the 84% quarterly ICR disclosure.

Risks and Concerns

  • "Health fee repricing...created a headwind for new sales and retention" (President, CEO & Director Simonds), with management also citing "a volatile business and geopolitical environment" and "the macroeconomic environment does remain volatile" (Executive VP & CFO Murthy).
  • "We do not expect to receive more benefit from the prior year." (Executive VP & CFO Murthy) "It's still early in the year" and "things happen" in claims costs, which management tied to the decision to maintain the full-year ICR range.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a stronger-than-expected start, tying profitability to completed health-fee repricing, favorable insurance development that was characterized as one-time, and continued expense discipline including a $14 million restructuring charge. Leadership reiterated full-year guidance ranges, while stating that Q1 insurance performance shifted full-year earnings expectations toward the top half of the guidance range, and highlighted near-term execution catalysts including the Cocoon acquisition, TriNet Global and TriNet IT partnerships, and early operational impacts from TriNet Assistant.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: General Electric wins $107.8M order under a multi-year defense framework
data.description:
• General Electric (GE) secured a ~$107.8M delivery order to supply J85 aircraft engines to the U.S. defense sector.
  • This order is part of a long-term defense contract (5 years 6 months base, extendable by 4 years 6 months), specifically covering Performance Period 5 with milestone-based lot payments tied to engine deliveries.
  • The current order is a one-year contract that does not include any option periods.
  • The deal is funded through the FY2025 defense budget and working capital funds, with the Defense Logistics Agency being the contracting activity.

data.title: Stryker in rare double earnings miss; maintains 2026 guidance
data.description:
• For the first time since at least Q1 2024 results, Stryker reported misses on both the top and bottom lines in its Q1 financial results.
  • However, the medtech maintained its 2026 guidance: Diluted EPS of $14.90 to $15.10 (consensus $14.96). Organic sales growth is projected at 8%-9.5%.
  • Stryker was hurt in the quarter by just a modest increase in net sales in Q1: the $6B figure was only a 2.6% increase.
  • Adjusted EPS also fell year over year to $2.60 from $2.84.
  • Stryker ended the quarter (March 31) with cash and cash equivalents of ~$2.9B compared to ~$4B on Dec. 31, 2025.

data.title: YD Bio Limited GAAP EPS of -$0.12, revenue of $0.6M
data.description:
• YD Bio Limited press release (YDES): FY GAAP EPS of -$0.12.
  • Revenue of $0.6M (+17.6% Y/Y).

data.title: U.S. wheat reverses lower after surging to near two-year high
data.description:
U.S. grain futures reversed course to finish lower Thursday following a retreat in crude oil prices and forecasts of improving weather across the central U.S.

On the Chicago Board of Trade, wheat (W_1:COM) for July delivery fell 2.3% to $6.36 3/4 per bushel after hitting its highest price since June 2024 in the previous session, and corn (C_1:COM) for July delivery added 0.6% to $4.74 3/4 per bushel after rising to a near one-year high, but July soybeans (S_1:COM) slipped 0.2% to $11.95 1/4 per bushel after reaching its best level ​in more than six weeks.

In April, CBOT wheat gained nearly 7%, corn climbed nearly 4%, and soybeans rose 2%.

Brent crude surged Thursday to a four-year high exceeding $126/bbl on fears that the Middle East war could see renewed military conflict and lead to a protracted supply disruption that could hurt global economic growth, but prices later ​turned around and finished lower.

The strength of oil prices, which have been rising due to supply disruptions linked to the war, has lifted the soy complex and corn futures, as soybeans and corn are feedstocks for biofuel.

Early planting of U.S. soybeans and corn ‌has progressed ⁠well, and forecasts for drier weather for the U.S. Corn Belt through this weekend eased some market concerns that storms could delay seeding in some areas, Reuters reported.

ETFs: (WEAT), (CORN), (SOYB), (DBA), (MOO)
